text: The surgeon begins adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions around the gallbladder using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon separates adhesions from the Hartmann's pouch, revealing a large stone that dilates the pouch. After removing the adhesions, the surgeon holds the body of the gallbladder.

The surgeon stretches the gallbladder anteromedially and uses harmonic or monopolar scissors to cut the Hartsman pouch. The gallbladder is split vertically, and the mucosa is opened to deliver the large stone.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully open its jaws in the proper plane, successfully separating and removing the stone from the surgical field.

The surgeon places the gallbladder on the transverse colon and greater omentum for stability. Suction irrigation is performed to remove spilled bile, preventing biliary peritonitis, before starting the dissection of Calot's triangle.
The surgeon observes the gallstone and proceeds to dissect the posterior peritoneum.
The surgeon applies anteromedial traction to the Hartmann's pouch, facilitating the separation of the posterior peritoneum.
The surgeon applies anterolateral traction to the gallbladder, facilitating the separation of the anterior peritoneum from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon separates the body of the gallbladder, achieving at least one-third separation, and forms a posterior window in the process.
The laparoscopic view shows the anatomical landmarks of Calot's triangle, with the common hepatic duct medially, the cystic duct laterally, and the inferior surface of the liver anterosuperiorly.

The surgeon identifies the liver surface and observes the posterior window, which is nicely formed with the gallbladder body separated by at least one-third. The liver is clearly visible, and the posterior window is established, allowing for the creation of an anterior window.
The gallbladder is visibly separated from the liver, with clear delineation of the posterior window and partial separation of the gallbladder body.
The surgeon forms the anterior window between the cystic duct and artery, completing the separation of the gallbladder body from the liver surface.
The surgeon separates the cystic pedicle, which involves carefully dissecting the connective tissue surrounding the cystic duct and artery within the previously formed anterior and posterior windows.
The surgeon skeletonizes the cystic duct and artery, preparing to tie an extracorporeal knot, such as a Miller's knot or Misra's knot, to secure these structures.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to feed a knot through the anterior window between the duct and artery, pulling the fibril multiple times to secure the extracorporeal knot around the continuous structure.
The surgeon extracts the tail of the suture from the surgical field, completing the ligation process.
The surgeon demonstrates the placement of a Misraj knot on the cystic duct and artery, securing the structures within the anterior window formed between them.
The surgeon creates the first hitch of the knot, securing the suture around the targeted structure.
The surgeon applies the first lock on the suture, securing the knot in place during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs the second wind of the suture technique, continuing the knot-tying process.
The surgeon completes the third vine and third log to form a secure knot, effectively ligating the cystic duct. The knot is visibly tightened to ensure secure closure.
The surgeon uses a Vanda Archer knot pusher to secure the knot on the cystic duct, ensuring tight ligation.
The surgeon cuts the suture after securing the cystic duct with a knot, completing the ligation process.
The surgeon applies a clip and knot to the cystic artery, securing it effectively during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies a titanium clip to the artery, securing it in place during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon lifts the horseman pouch to maintain exposure while cutting the cystic duct approximately three to five millimeters above the secure knot.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to coagulate the artery, facilitating the separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed. The remaining steps of the cholecystectomy proceed as typical.

The surgeon applies anterolateral and anteromedial traction to separate the shoulders of the gallbladder, exposing the peritoneal folds. Subsequently, the body of the gallbladder is maneuvered into an areolar plane for further separation.
The surgeon uses the left hand to provide anterolateral and anteromedial traction, aiding in the separation of the gallbladder from the liver.

With half of the gallbladder separated from the liver, the surgeon adjusts the light cable of the thirty-degree telescope downward to enhance visibility of the fossa, revealing its distinct coloration.
The surgeon separates the gallbladder from the liver, continuing the dissection process to fully detach the gallbladder.
The surgeon performs fulguration to control any oozing observed in the surgical field during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon reaches the final section of the gallbladder, preparing to complete its separation from the liver bed.
The surgeon performs blunt dissection to separate the remaining tissue, then uses a commercially available endo bag to transfer the gallbladder and any stones into the bag for removal.

The surgeon places a large stone into a commercially available endo bag, crushing it inside to prevent any stone fragments from contaminating the port wound and minimizing the risk of abscess formation.
The endo bag is positioned inside the abdominal cavity, ready to receive the gallbladder and any stones.
The surgeon opens the mouth of the endo bag and transfers the gloves and stone into the bag, ensuring containment within the surgical field.
A large stone is observed slipping within the surgical field, potentially requiring repositioning or containment.
The surgeon uses a grasper to manipulate the stone and gallbladder, facilitating their transfer into the opened endo bag within the surgical field.
The surgeon drops the gallbladder stone into the endo bag and proceeds to crush the stone within the bag using appropriate instruments.
The surgeon uses a stone holding forceps to crush the gallbladder stone, visibly breaking it into smaller fragments.
The surgeon removes the stone holding forceps and introduces a claw forceps into the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a claw forceps through the epigastric port to grasp the edges of the endo bag, pulling it into the cannula with a semi-traumatic grip.

The surgeon uses ovum forceps to break the stone, which is then removed through the cannula. Following this, thorough lavage, suction, and irrigation are performed before removing and closing the surgical ports.
